item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
since someone else has probably already made the joke of giving this article a quantum superpositioned updown vote, I'll instead just say that this:

"Plate appeared empty. Clever choice - minimalist. Upon closer inspection, noted area of gaseous mixture immediately above plate: roughly 78% N / 21% O. Inspired - if must imitate, imitate one of the greats. Inhaled mixture; amazing effects: transmission of oxygen across the membranes of lungs and into bloodstream. Extended my lifespan (anomalous effect?). Summary: would def. breathe again."

Deserves to be on the freaking -J list. and I mean that as a complement.

Although I did notice something. before I voted I could refresh to flip between the versions easily, but after I voted I could only view the page inverse to whichever vote I gave it, with cancelling my vote leaving it in whichever state I last left it. Is this intentional?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that this article has two iterations, the sourcing information will be split into the two separate iterations.

Iteration Q:
The first image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed. The second image is too specific to not have been made for the article, and thus is assumed to have been made for the article and is cc-compliant.

Iteration W:
The first image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it has been found on multiple stock photo sites (such as iStock), which require purchase for official use, making it non-compliant. Thus, the image has been removed. The second image is too specific to not have been made for the article, and thus is assumed to have been made for the article and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must admit, it took me a while to notice this article's 'special feature'. However it feels more like an anomalous item than a fully-fledged SCP. A lot of effort has gone into this though by the looks of it so I'm going to upvote even if I'm not keen of the SCP's idea itself.

Edit: Well done for +100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was sure that it had blatant tone issues until I got the catch. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user is permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked it, when I first read it it was in the enthusiastic state, only after reading the comments I realized it has two states. I refreshed about ten times before finding the other version, the picture of the angry face in the non-enthusiastic state was very funny.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I started reading this on my phone but I went out of charge half through it. 8 hours later I opened it again but this time on my PC. I almost crapped my pants off when I realized that I was reading a complete inverse version of the article. I really though I was going crazy or I just dreamed the first version lol. Nice work dude

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a fun read, even if I've seen that particular type of format screw before.

As an aside, I'm rather horrified that the Foundation uses D-class personnel with the developmental age of 6-year-olds… 0_0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
gr8 b8 m8 wud r8 8/8

I was expecting something like it, and then it came with that cute carrot picture.

Didn't even realize the random switching showing the "shabby" version =O this is cute as hell

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was, at first, going to critique you for tone. But then I got to reading the whole thing and wow! Nice work. I really enjoy the way it shifts and affects the article, though a small part of me doesn't want it to be neutralized.

Perhaps, though maintaining the sarcastic/eloquent tone, you could make its properties more clear? Just a thought. It took me 2 read-throughs to get it.

Overall, though? Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woo! Thanks mate!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If anyone who came late to this one wants to see the "pleasure for your eyes!!!!" faux-document-takeover page that was originally reachable via one of the normal iterations, it can be found right here on the Sandbox. (Please note that this page is no longer part of this entry! I link to it as a couple of people have asked me for it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mr Carbon, I only love you more for what you've accomplished. If it helps any, your skip is like Skip himself: Commentary on what we do here and why we do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's built around being a gimmick in a box, which is architecture, not literature. Null vote because the bit that isn't gimmick (being unable to write about it objectively) is neat, well-written, and (given the context) tonally correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, shouting "Fooled you!" definitely isn't fun, and wasn't the point of the exercise. I consider that side of things to be an undesirable, yet unavoidable, side effect of trying this idea out. I certainly didn't revel in it. (The biggest thing I failed to predict was that some people would object to the 'reveal' so strongly. That was a useful thing to learn in terms of future article plans.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted because I like the effect, but I would upvote again after reading the comments.
Congratulations on a successful social engineering, Mr. Carbon.

Much like the bad-old days of AWCY? glut, this appeals to me because even those who hate it are unintentionally playing along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too clever by half :/ Shouting "Fooled you!" at the end of the day is no fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ninja'd by Scantron. In fairness, it's a reasonable conclusion to reach if you haven't got any other evidence. "Oh, sure, MrCarbon, you meant to get a low-single-digits vote. Pshyeah, right." That's why I made sure to let a few people in on it in advance (Piffy, implied to Scantron, and I can't remember who else). Who, I'm sure, would never have let me hear the end of it if the article had ended up bombing :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They actually WERE just as planned — MrCarbon informed several people beforehand to avoid insinuations like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… only saw this now, so the comment thread is somewhat baffling…

The article as it stands is just entertaining enough for an upvote, though it's a bit of a tired concept. The meta shit apparently going on with regards to screwing with the reader and claiming that the downvotes were just as planned! comes off a little annoying, but hey, my vote is based on what's in the article now (and I am not really sure exactly what was going on beforehand), so it stands either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, they're both Alan Rickman, so I'm almost not alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I didn't reply, because… I mean. Of all the comments anyone made on this article, yours was the one I expected the least.

Note to self: in future, proof drafts for intimation of Hot Pocket existence…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe: The Foundation doesn't know. The creator designed it with built-in obsolescence for reasons known only to him.

Out-of-universe: I thought it'd be fitting.

E: Got lazy and didn't reference SNDG as a singular (the "group" is actually just one guy). Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*sniffles*

Apparently I'm the only one who mistook the folded napkin on the finer plate to be a hotpocket.

*is sad*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My, my, Montreal is really becoming a hotbed of SCPs. It took me a while to get a good grasp of this one, and as probabilities would have it, I decided to give it a +1 for enjoyment. It seemed a bit like a jumble of elements that feel like they have already been done; made me think a bit of the toaster gag. But I like the idea of this uncertainty principle of reviews and it was enjoyable to read. The food element made me think of that story by Woody Allen: http://www.e-reading.mobi/chapter.php/111264/18/Allen_-_Side_Effects.html
''I began my meal with an antipasto, which at first appeared aimless, but as I focused more on the anchovies the point of it became clearer. Was Spinelli trying to say that all life was represented here in this antipasto, with the black olives an unbearable reminder of mortality?''

I am not sure why pictures are different in each iteration? The way I see it, the reviews, including the SCP document itself, can be perceived as either positive or negative by the reader, but why is the actual picture of the object different? Is the object itself perceived in either of these two states or does this only occur for pictures that are used as illustrations for a ''review''?

Did anybody else read the negative article in Snape's voice?

Personally, I thought more about Marvin from the Hitchiker's Guide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also note that, like your predictions, the current rating is now at +20.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this suddenly neutralized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the whole meta-thing was very cleverly done, although I do not approve of its ultimate goal.

However, I can only vote on the content of the article, not something that happened to it or because of it or in its discussion thread. And the content is nothing new, with or without the page-switching gimmick. As such, the downvote stays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this diverges enough from the minotaur story now that the comparisons are lacking. Yes, it uses the same itteration trick, but what it now presents is a very different beast. The minotaur story has several completely different variations, with the only constant being the standard article content. Here we have this play on perceptions. We have 2 completely parallel versions of the article, with a kind of dipole effect. I like the fact that the documents use the same experiements, but change the results for different viewings. It plays into a sort of quantum mechanical uncertainty thing that works well.

Did anybody else read the negative article in Snape's voice?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, but it takes a second read to really get it

Upvote from me

item.title: zomg what a genius idea!!!
item.content:encoded: Just kidding, have your upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That depends on whether my goal was:

1. To get as many upvotes as possible, or

2. To try out an experiment that I, and possibly some others, found interesting.

Obviously, not everyone is going to find the latter interesting. Some will find it infuriating. That comes with the territory, so I just have to suck that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sound and fury signifying nothing. You put a ton of effort into something deliberately mediocre. I don't know what to tell you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the point isn't that anyone who previously downvoted will suddenly go 'zomg what a genius idea!!!' and upvote unto infinity. I considered the article an experiment, rather than a gimmick - as a 'gimmick' would imply I was doing things for the sole purpose of getting a bunch of cheap upvotes, which was expressly the opposite of the goal - but I knew it'd probably still split opinion even when fully explained. Ah well. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, now it's even more gimmicky, in a meta sense rather than a wiki-modifying sense? Yeah, think I'm keeping my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Anyone coming to the article after this point: if you're confused, please see my new post at the top of the thread.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Supplement and page linking to it link no longer exist - see my post at the top of this thread for the explanation :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean, you suspect that I did that on purpose? How dare you, varlet? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please note that as of the edit outlined in this post, only iterations 'q' and 'w' still exist. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note: This item has been edited from its deliberately contentious initial form into something a bit more subuded. This was a planned edit and is not due to feedback. Comments made prior to about 0800 EDT on 8 October pertain to the previous version.

Later on when I have more time, I may throw something on the Leaks thread to explain the execution in full, but the tl;dr version is as follows: as Waterfire and a couple of other people realized, a major part of the original version of this skip was intended to be the extension of the 'polarizing' effect to the Wiki voter base. Many aspects of the entry - from the 'lolsupplement' to the way in which the random effect was implemented - were deliberately selected in order to invoke this effect.

I didn't want that version of the article to last forever, so I set its duration to be about 24 hours, figuring that if I failed badly that'd be when it was deleted anyway. As it was, the votes at that time were hovering in the single-digit pluses with about 50 total voters, which was close enough to a polarized vote for me to declare a qualified success. (As I told a couple of people before I posted this, my ideal result would have been an average of 0 after 24 hours with 100 votes or more.)

PS: I'm only replying here because I wasn't quick enough to get the first post on my own thread. D'oh!

EDIT: If anyone wants to see the 'anomalously defaced' page that could be found from one of the prior iterations, it can be found on the sandbox here. Please note that this is no longer part of this article! I provide it simply so that those who didn't catch the time-limited original version can still see the eye damage wonderful artistic statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In fairness, the supplement is linked from one iteration of this page, in place of the usual test log collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm of two minds about it.

(edit: Actually, I really like it, but I was hoping this'd start a pun thread)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been letting this discussion run, but I do want to chime in here: the article (on several of its pages) states that the polarization is between enthusiasm and dismissiveness, not enthusiasm and hatred. :) (That's why Dr. Katzen isn't cursing up a storm, and why the D-class just scribbled out an attempt at a drawing as if the object wasn't even worth the time, etc…) This was a deliberate choice for purely aesthetic reasons, simply because I find sarcastically dismissive polemic more entertaining than 'gah I hate this eff this it effin' sucks'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is 100% reliant on the gimmick. Gimmicks enhance articles, they don't make them. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I don't think this needs multiple iterations (and I don't see at a glance why there are so many, unless they're divided by the addenda). The version I read was perfectly enjoyable on its own merits, as a format-screw (we haven't had one of those in a while!) that also provides humorous commentary on tone issues in articles. I like it for that, I'm upvoting for that.

EDIT: Looking into it a bit more, yeah, it just seems like permutations of the test logs. In some ways worth it, in some ways really not. (Although I really love the one with the empty plate. So carbon dioxide, such breath.) The French page is just wat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The gimmick is basically The Minotaur's Tale and without the gimmick, it's just kinda meh. Also, I'm REALLY not digging the supplement, nor that it isn't linked to/from this page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who thought it was a hotpocket on the pretty plate and setting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just don't see anything interesting with it, maybe others do, but I'll pass

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems that people are quite polarized about this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then it might actually fit, but at the moment, I don't see it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"As the item is so profoundly uninteresting, it is envisaged that few will ever bother to test it. However, should no drying paint be available for viewing, SCP-1496 may be placed in a humanoid containment cell along with whichever unfortunate drew the short straw as the participant. Something to put on SCP-1496-1 may be thrown in, if you must. Note that no living being is to be placed on SCP-1496-1 without Senior Researcher approval, which is the only approval SCP-1496 will ever get."

Classic.

Now, I think for -J it would have to be changed such that only the negative review appears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't seem that j-ish…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just reread the containment procedures on the negative review of the plate. They're so funny, I was goin to quote them, but I ended up quoting the whole thing. Seriously, if this dies, I think it should at LEAST be a -J.

As for people complaining about the format screw, how does it screw with the "typical format"? Unlike Minotaur or the crossword puzzle, this still has a clear object class, description, etc. It has the proper format! It's just that the contents happen to change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
exactly
uninteresting is a neutral word, people won't get strongly polarized on something that is simply 'meh'. People will have extreme emotions of love and hatred on issues because they are part of those peoples' interests. You're not going to have people fight over something they agree is uninteresting because they don't have the slightest reason to care about it. This, on the other hand seems to make people highly biased, which totally contradicts the statement that it's uninteresting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That doesn't contradict the meaning of the previous sentence.
It just says that it is impossible to write any kind of record in a dispassionate way. Either you hate it or love it, and you will write with passion about the plate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The main reason I said that is because the very next sentence says

it is impossible to make any sort of record of SCP-1496 that does not have the flavor of an opinionated review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone is (tones are?) intentional and a part of the object's anomalous effect. It says right in the description that it's impossible to make a record of SCP-1496 without it taking the form of an opinionated (positive or negative) review. I was initially about to downvote immediately when I read the words "SCP-1496 […] is the single most appealing item the Foundation has yet contained." but it was so over-the-top throughout the article that I realized there had to be a reason for it.

I ended up downvoting anyway because, like others have said, the article doesn't really do enough with the format screw to justify its use. But I don't have an issue with the tone.

As for your second point, I just don't see how "uninteresting" would make more sense in context. That sentence is from the negative-review version of the description. It's saying that the plate is so dull and boring that nothing could ever possibly be done to make it interesting. If you say "it's impossible to make it uninteresting" then you're saying that it's always interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still don't enjoy it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone is kind of the point of the article. Read the whole thing.
